speaker: interviewer
text: Gringlas. And Joseph, we were talking about you and your brother, Sol, went off to work at night, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was working at night, and he was day shift. So he was-- at that time, they took out all the Jewish people to send away. He was at that in [? market, ?] but he was on the list that he works in the factory. So they took him out from the other people which were on the list and working in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came down evening, because it took all day long till they got all of these people together in the market. And he told me about what happened. I knew exactly what-- what's gonna-- what's gonna happen, but he was he was there. He saw it. And-- and then when he came down, I was starting to cry at that time. I couldn't stop it. It was for hours. How-- how did you-- how did you know what was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we had a feeling. People were talking that they're gonna surround the town. But this wasn't the first time they did to our town. We had other towns that were liquidated. So they-- little by little, they get every city surrounded. We knew. We had a feeling something is going to happen. And well, they used a lot Ukrainian in black uniforms to help those German Nazis to do their job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they rounded people up, what-- what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did they do? They-- what they did is send them in on the trains to send them out. There was destination Majdanek or Treblinka. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that was the destination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we-- we-- people knew exactly-- hear about what's going on. You know? They heard about it. But because this-- our town wasn't the first started. It was starting other towns. If something comes out, people start telling and find out that they're liquidating the Jewish people out of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you heard this news, you knew you had lost your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I thought-- then that night, when I'd been to work at night, I knew it-- I looked at my parents-- I knew this was the  last time I'm gonna see them. Because I know we working, they'll keep us for awhile. But they would be sent away right away. We knew what they were doing with the people. At the time, it's been a few years after-- in the war going in, and we knew exactly what's happening to the people, Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any reports later on about what happened to your family members? After they were-- when they were transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing what happened-- you know, I told you when we were in the factory after they liquidated Jewish people, and then we didn't have no place to stay in the factory so they-- they took us back to the city where we used to live in like a little ghetto from the workers. And we came-- there was a lot of people still, you know, from-- so they told us that my father-- they saw my father and mother and my sister. They were hidden for a few days, but they couldn't exist without eating. And-- so they had to go out. And when we came in the-- in the city from the factory, we had to stay in that little town. So they-- somebody told us they saw them sending them away. That's what happened. So just a few hours before we went to the [? market-- ?] to the-- to the place where we-- they let us live there from the factory, so we found out that they sent them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- your older brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Older brothers were sent right away. The older ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the same transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- I don't think the same transport, because I told you my parents were hidden with-- a few days and then they came out. There's no food, so they came out and so they didn't-- they weren't together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out about your brother, your older brother and his baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: We found out they were all going-- they sent them away. They took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife too? How much longer did you stay in a ghetto after that incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we stayed in the ghetto that-- in going every morning, they took us with guards to the factory. You know. And we stayed there about a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was like a-- they gave you a little bit of soup and a piece of bread. That was the whole-- what they gave us. But one thing about when we worked in the factory, we had work with Gentiles. They worked in the factory, too. So some people, you know, they help you out with some food. But to me, it happened later on something different, which I'll tell you later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked in the factory for a few more months--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then they started to make appell in that little ghetto. They made an appell, which means all-- all had to get out on a place and stay in the line. So we stand out, and they needed some people for another camps. And I didn't know they got-- they took me out from the line, separate me from my brother. So this was a terrible shock to me, because I didn't have any-- I was young, and I lost my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about 15, 16. 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sent to Blizyn, which is-- was a 50 kilometer from my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We transport there I think on-- I think by trucks. I don't exactly remember. By trucks, I think. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you told Blizyn was? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Blizyn was-- it was terrible. This was-- and we were starting there in that camp. We-- to build barracks for other people to come in. And-- and we saw the SS surrounding, and the guards were black uniform Ukrainians guarding Blizyn. And what started-- every time the appell. They took out from the appell. There was woods around, and they were killing them in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, going-- excuse me-- going back before I was into Blizyn, and with the appell I told you. So what I remember exactly what happened, they-- before they took us-- people to send to Blizyn. This was not too long after the liquidation. A few days, you know, I told you. And there was-- we standing. An SS man was standing in front of me. It was [? full ?] and the [? third-- ?] a [INAUDIBLE] in their uniform. And it was something came out, a little kid. It was about a year and a half, or two years-- walked out of no place. We didn't know what happened. A kid walked out. Probably a run-- got away or something. We didn't know what happened. So the SS man saw the kid walk around [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was on a place with garbage. So he was telling the kid to come to the garbage. He took out a gun. He was killing him. We were standing in a line seeing that. The baby was about two years, and he just walked over. [INAUDIBLE]. Was this is the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the ghetto before they send me to Blizyn, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to Blizyn--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were making barracks there you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were building barracks there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were conditions like in Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blizyn was terrible. The-- and the SS and the screaming, like with dogs and-- it was terrible. I just felt like-- you-- you felt like that's the end of it, you know? That's-- you won't ever get out of there. And there was a [? steinbrook, ?] as I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is what is a [? steinbrook? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The [? steinbrook ?] was something breaking stones for roads to build. And so I worked in that [? steinbrook. ?] In [? the steinbrook, ?] we'd have to go out of the camp with the guards, the SS. And it was about a half a mile, a mile away. And the-- [? one ?] SS, and mostly what they used SS in those camps-- they were injured on the front or something, and they had to give them something to keep busy so they send those to death camps. Can you imagine those who were injured a little bit that the one-- one SS were screaming like terrible. And when you hear the screaming, gave me a feeling like terribly he felt. Like you could get a gun out and kill you-- anything. It's terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think-- how-- how did you manage to survive that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I can not believe how I survived. I don't know. It's-- it's-- it was terrible. We tried to-- when we saw the kitchen, grab potatoes, getting-- stealing from the kitchen some to help yourself. I remember one thing that happened in Blizyn, I was sick. I got sick-- typhus. It was-- and they put you into a barrack special for the sick. And I don't know how I got-- some people, a lot of people died from sickness, and I got out about a week later. I got out of this, because if you were there, you know they're not gonna survive because they're going to kill right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I got-- felt better. But after typhus, I was so hungry. I couldn't imagine. You could climb up a [? pole, ?] if you were hungry. So what I did, I-- I took from the kitchen with-- they bring in potatoes. I stole a few potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then what happened-- and I went in-- it was a-- like a something, empty building. And one of the guys from my hometown, he had made a fire, and we put down on the brick the few potatoes. And I look in the front, and the head of the SS in the front of me. So what I did-- I-- he was so tall, I run. I ran through his feet. I [? did ?] run. I was there. I couldn't run no place else, but I got so scared. And what happened in the evening, the appell, they called-- if anything happened, they had an appell. I told you what appell is, standing all from the camps in lines. And I told you I had a number, 167. And they called-- called up, and I thought they're going to take me into the woods to be killed. But the head of the-- I remember his name was Nel, N-E-L. He was the head of-- head of SS in Blizyn. And he said I should get 24 on my back, 24 lashes. So they-- and we had in our-- it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you get 24 lashes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and that was-- if not that, I would have to-- usually, that something that happened, that somebody gave him the idea of telling this to do to me. Because mostly when something bad happen, they took the people out to be killed in the woods. So I had to lay down on a chair, and there were the head of the Jewish policeman in that Blizyn. One from one side and other side, and I got 25 on my rear. And then after that happened, I was bleeding like from my tush, that-- that he's like, you putting potatoes in the ground was like [INAUDIBLE]. I was laying down in the barracks. I was crying, screaming. That was what happened at that time. I remember exactly what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was 15, 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you lie about your age in order to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I lied to my age and that happened. And then after Blizyn was liquidated, they sent us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blizyn, I would say it was-- every day looked like a year, but I think it must have been there about close to a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked outside in the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked outside in the winter in Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we worked outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of uniforms did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time was not like-- it was like civilians, but you had to carry a number. My number was under-- underneath with a piece of cloth with a number on. And that's what they [INAUDIBLE]. That was in Blizyn, the numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- did you have a coat to wear in the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sleeping on-- in the barracks. Was just like in woods, and there was like a thin blanket. That's what we got. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about when you had to work outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: They get-- there was no-- nothing. Was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you left Blizyn, where were you sent? How-- how were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Blizyn, when they liquidated Blizyn, so they send us to Auschwitz in the trains. And we-- we were packed in the trains from the Blizyn to Auschwitz, and the train went through the woods near Kielce, not far from my hometown. And we would hear shooting at the train. That was the partisan shooting at the trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we thought we were going to be liberated, but there was not enough power then. The SS were shooting with automatic weapons back. Nothing happened, and eventually, we got to Blizyn. Now I remind myself-- you asked me how we went to Blizyn. It was by train. When I start telling you about the shooting at the train, then, yeah, by train. And when you were transported from Blizyn to Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what time-- what type of year was it? Was it winter or summer? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- to Auschwitz? I think it was-- was in summer. It was May or June.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- at the beginning-- beginning of 1944, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or maybe before. It's hard to remember the years, but it was-- it-- it was-- oh, maybe the end of summer 1943. It was when they liquidated Blizyn. Couldn't tell, because they have-- they have dates when it was liquidated. I found out all the dates where it was-- from on-- from on the camp [INAUDIBLE] when they liquidated people, they found out from archives that when it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were transported from Blizyn to Auschwtiz, how long did it take for you get there? How long were you on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On train, we were quite a few hours and all packed like sardines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given anything to drink or food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no food. No drinks. But this was just a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what happened when you arrived? Did you arrive in Auschwitz? Or in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I arrived in Birkenau, and they opened the-- opened the trains. And the SS with-- with-- with the dogs, screaming, and they-- and they-- they took us from Blizyn-- I think they took us for work. They assumed that we worked in Blizyn that we gonna work. So I don't know if they took the whole transport for work or not, but mostly other transport came, right away they didn't-- they didn't take them to work. They just sent them to the crematorium. But we were assigned to work, but there was nothing work. We just cleaned Birkenau. There wasn't a factory to work for people. It just was a waiting station to be sent to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you told this, or-- or how did you know you were waiting to be killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, what-- what we remember there was appells. I told you about appells, putting appells, counting to make sure that there-- there's nobody disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what the appells are again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what that is again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Appell is something-- all from the barracks at a certain time in the evening or in the morning, was do twice. In the morning out and the evening, to count every-- to count everyone is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long would this take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this takes-- some appell would take for-- for quite a few hours, to-- especially in winter time. They tried to keep us in the [INAUDIBLE], to be frozen. So what I was telling you-- excuse me. [DRINKS] So they came in Auschwitz, I told you. They'd unload the wagons, and all the people jumped out. [GERMAN]-- you hear these German's screaming, [SPEAKING GERMAN]!

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means get out fast. Get out fast. And there was somebody already from there before they were there in Auschwitz. They told us what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no some people would-- they were before coming to Auschwitz, and they helped opening up the wagons. They told us what-- what's going on there. And we saw what happened. At night, we saw four crematoriums, and the sky was all red from burning-- from burning. Smoke coming out of the chimneys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was appell [INAUDIBLE] appell was, and a kapo. A kapo was always picked-- from our people was picked-- he should-- they knew he was a strong guy. He can hurt somebody, can hit-- hit somebody, so they made him a kapo. And he said, the kapo, he-- and he said to us, if you have anything hidden or something, gold or anything, give it out. You see the chimney there? That where you're going to come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was a Jewish guy, a kapo, telling us that this is-- this is it. You're not coming out from here alive. Whatever you have, give it out, because other-- you're going to come out of the chimney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you told this when you first got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, first-- that first few days we come out. If you have anything hidden, we should give it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bring any possessions with you to Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have possession, anything. But some people probably had something. Who knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you given a uniform when you arrived in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when we come in Birkenau, and we were given like uniform. There was stripes, blue and white stripes uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you issued a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- yeah. A few days later. We-- probably those people where they took the crematorium, they didn't have-- give them a chance, any numbers. They didn't care. But the people they kept us wait-- holding out a while, they gave us a number,

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what-- where did they give you a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- we-- they put an appell, and there was somebody going through with a pen or something hitting in the numbers on the arm. Depends-- you give an arm on this side, they put it other side. I turn around, I got my number on this side.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a tattoo on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a tattoo on my arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what number were you given?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's B2247.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you were given your number, were you put to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was just cleaning. Doing the work-- there was one thing happen that there was-- people were standing and doing nothing. And so they-- they wanted to make it a little bit-- look nicer. They came out and ask for somebody's a gardener. And I thought find anything to ask for it, and I was-- I had interest in school in Poland to be doing some gardening. We learned about it. So I said, I can do it. And right at that time, I got a double portion of butter and double portion of bread, which was very helpful. But then when the people standing around, a lot of people-- they stepped on it. And the next few days they said, if you want to take care of it, if you want to work on that, you have to take something and hit him over the head. So I-- I couldn't do that. That wasn't me. And I had to give up. And I didn't want [INAUDIBLE] to be hitting people and [? things. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you gave that up, where-- where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So just standing, doing what other people. Cleaning, do other things. But-- but it was taken away, the portion that I had doubled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you punished for giving up the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like if you don't want to do this kind, doing what they told you to, hitting, and then you're not gonna get more-- more eat-- food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Sol during this time? Was he with you in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sol-- I told you, I was separated from my brother in my hometown. He was still working now in Ostrowiec in that factory, and I was sent to Blizyn. So by the time they liquidated Blizyn, they liquidated my hometown, too. And he was sent-- he was in Auschwitz, too. He was in Birkenau, but I didn't know about him. He didn't know about me. But he was sent to Buna. Buna is eight kilometers from Auschwitz, from Birkenau, and Buna was the big factories. The Germans had all factories that would make ammunition called IG Farben. That was the name of the Buna. And what happened to me after a few months being in Birkeanu, they-- it was a Sunday, and they took us from Auschwitz Birkenau in appell. And I was-- that-- taken out, too, and we put in-- in a barrack. And we all naked, stripped naked completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what we waiting-- we was waiting for somebody to come, SS, to check us out. And at the evening it was-- came a few SS, and they had Dr. Mengele. Josef Mengele was there. How'd I know it Mengele? That voice or the pictures, and I remember exactly him. Never got out of my eyes when I saw him. So what we had to do-- it was a long barracks. In the middle of the barracks was that brick. You couldn't go across, you had to go around, all around to the edge. He was standing in line, and he was on one side putting on headlights. And we had to go around and take up the hands. He'd look around and see if we had any scars, or anything sick or what. In any case, when I came to him, and he ask me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? That means how old are you? I said I'm 18 years old. He asked me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Can you work? I said, [GERMAN]. I can-- I can work. I can-- [GERMAN]. So he put me to the place that they're going to be sent away. So what happened, the people that were selected they sent to Buna. What happened from Buna when a lot of people couldn't work, they brought them to Auschwitz to be sent to the crematoriums. What happened to the people that night after you were selected to go to Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Those who were not selected--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken to ovens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were more people selected--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to go to ovens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because they didn't take us to work, so the old-- mostly people waiting, well, that was the end in Auschwitz and Birkenau. But we were-- I was selected to go to Buna. In the time [INAUDIBLE] Buna, I met my brother after a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you met or saw Dr. Mengele, were you-- how close were you to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was close. I'd say a few feet away. I mean, two or three feet, that's all. Because we had to go close. He looked at us like, you know, you look up at a person, how he is, so we were very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very handsome man and very cool. Black, dark eyes. He was nice officer, straight and there was a few SS around with him. And that's when he asked the question.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were sent then to Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Buna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long then had you been at Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Birkeanu, I'd say, a few months, five months or maybe four months. I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to Buna, how were you transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- Buna, I think we were transported in trucks, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of you were transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was quite a few hundred that went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when did you meet up with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was like in the movies. You couldn't believe it. But it was-- I-- I was [? shocked ?] and then we saw again. When I started to see him, we grabbed at each other and we crying. I couldn't separate [? his side. ?] It was a [INAUDIBLE] moment. It's a moment to never forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you want the truck on your way to Buna when you saw your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was in Buna already. Yeah. We were already assigned to a barrack already in Buna. So he came-- he heard that people came in from Auschwitz, so he wanted to see and we met again. But he-- he-- he was a tailor. So what happened in the-- there was a lot of-- in-- in-- in Buna-- kapos that were not Jewish. They German's murderers. Murdered-- they were Gentiles, but they murdered. Instead of sending them to jail, they sent them to take care of us and being a kapo. And the other ones-- there were other ones with red-- red markings, just like Commie or something, you know. But those murderers were terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what kinds of things would they do? What-- what did they do as kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were hitting. They were, you know, one kapo came out one time, and the commander was assigned was commander number was [GERMAN]. That means two. Number two is in German [GERMAN]. And then I worked outside, very hard work, and then he-- he asked if somebody can take care of socks, knit socks. And I had-- [LAUGHS] I knew something about it in school. I had learned. So he brought his socks that had torn, and I knitted and he took-- there, the kapos had like a little thing built from wood. They was going inside. They were staying inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in, and he gave me food, the kapo. And he was a murderer with a green sign, and he gave me food. And I thought, oh, I got something to [? help ?] here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then a few nights being there, he came-- he came in back. And he started screaming at me, going crazy. And he-- he was sent-- he told me to get out of there. And the same kapo took me to [INAUDIBLE], carry logs and shoulders. And I was sick. I was a temperature that time. I couldn't believe in a person how he changed. He I saw I was darning his socks. He was helping with a little bit more food and then suddenly became strange. He was hitting me. He was starting to get out of that. And he-- he put me-- he make sure that I [? working ?] [? hard ?] carrying those logs on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened, I was-- got so sick, and I was sent in Buna to krankenbau. That means sick house. And when I went up on the-- in kranken house, there were Hungarian doctors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened, I was climbing up to go. I was on the second bed to go up. The bed is wood, and I fell down and I fainted. So I hit myself, but those Hungarians doctors saved my life. They-- they got me back. I was-- I fainted. They got me back, and when I woke up, I was full bandage [INAUDIBLE]. My brother saw me next day I was out, after a few days out, so I bandaged. I told him what happened to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and then I was assigned to the [INAUDIBLE]. We didn't go back to the same murder company that I worked for. I was assigned to a different company. I got [? helped ?] to work with some other people. It wasn't so hard work. What-- what kind of work were you doing then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other one was like mechanical and something with metal, keys or something-- like, something like metal things to do. It was not hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now where you and your brother in the same barracks at Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was in separate barrack. He was a tailor, and so he was sewing things for those kapos, for the German kapos. They were, as I told you, criminals. But-- so he had a little bit more food. And one time, I remember, I was-- in-- one of the kapos knew that I'm his brother. And he put me to work to put-- load something those wagons, the little wagons. And I know, he came to me, the kapo, and he hit me with a shovel in face. I was bleeding. I said what the heck. He-- he knows I'm a brother to Sol, and he sews for him things. But that was a little bit crazy. I was bleeding. He hit me with a shovel. [INAUDIBLE] So true. Excuse me.
